coredata.dc:title: Novel quaternized carbon dots modified polysulfone-based anion exchange membranes with improved performance
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2019 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Yufei
Hu, Bo
Tong, Cuiyan
Bai, Yang
Lü, Changli
coredata.dc:description: At present, low conductivity and poor chemical stability are still the biggest challenges in the research on anion exchange membranes (AEMs). Herein, novel nanocomposite AEMs were first constructed by introducing quaternized carbon dots (QCDs) into the imidized polysulfone matrix (Im-PSU). QCDs were synthesized by quaternization of CDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine. The physicochemical properties and electrochemical properties of the nanocomposite AEMs were significantly improved due to the introduction of QCDs. It was found that the QCDs can improve the ion transport channel of the nanocomposite AEMs. Compared with pure Im-PSU AEM, the OH− conductivity and physicochemical properties of the nanocomposite membranes were enhanced, and the OH− conductivity of ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs composite membrane can reach 109.3 mS cm−1 at 80 °C, and 61.2% initial OH− conductivity was maintained in 1.0 M NaOH solution for 500 h at 60 °C. Our research proves that the nanofiller with a small size can better improve the performance of composite AEMs, and provide an efficient strategy for future research work in the design and preparation of AEMs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anion exchange membranes
Imidized polysulfone
Quaternized carbon dots
Nanocomposite
Improved properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Ion exchange capacity (IEC), water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SR) and hydration number (λ)
1H NMR spectra of CMPSU and Im-PSU
IEC, WU, SR and hydration number(λ)

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2019 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: 1H NMR spectra of (a) PSU, CMPSU and (b) Im-PSU.
FTIR spectra of (a) CDs and QCDs, and (b) different AEMs; (c) 1H NMR spectra of CDs and QCDs, and (d) TGA curves of CDs and QCDs.
(a) Low resolution and (b) high resolution TEM images, (c) particle size distribution histogram of QCDs and (d) XPS full spectra of CDs and QCDs.
(a, d) High resolution XPS spectra of C 1s for CDs and QCDs. (b, e) High resolution XPS spectra of N 1s for CDs and QCDs. (c, f) High resolution XPS spectra of O 1s for CDs and QCDs.
TEM images of (a) ImPSU, (b) ImPSU-0.5%-QCDs, (c) ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs, and (d) ImPSU-5.0%-QCDs.
SEM images of (a) ImPSU, (b) ImPSU-0.5%-QCDs, (c) ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs, and (d) ImPSU-5.0%-QCDs.
TGA curves and mechanical properties of different AEMs.

Dependence of (a) anion conductivities and (b) IEC and water absorption on the content of QCDs for different AEMs. (c) Relationship between IEC, water uptake and ionic conductivity of different AEMs. (d) Arrhenius curves of different AEMs.
Hydroxide conductivity as a function of IECs of different AEMs at 80 °C.

Alkaline resistance of different membranes evaluated in 1.0 M NaOH solution at 60 °C: (a) the variation trend in hydroxide conductivity, and (b) the percentage curve of residual hydroxide conductivity of different AEMs.
Ion transport mechanism in ImPSU-x-QCDs membranes.
IEC, WU, SR, λ and oxidative stability of membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The samples were measured at room temperature.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The time when the membrane began to break.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The time when the membrane broke into pieces.

head.ce:title: Novel quaternized carbon dots modified polysulfone-based anion exchange membranes with improved performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun, 130024, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: At present, low conductivity and poor chemical stability are still the biggest challenges in the research on anion exchange membranes (AEMs). Herein, novel nanocomposite AEMs were first constructed by introducing quaternized carbon dots (QCDs) into the imidized polysulfone matrix (Im-PSU). QCDs were synthesized by quaternization of CDs derived from citric acid and ethylenediamine. The physicochemical properties and electrochemical properties of the nanocomposite AEMs were significantly improved due to the introduction of QCDs. It was found that the QCDs can improve the ion transport channel of the nanocomposite AEMs. Compared with pure Im-PSU AEM, the OH− conductivity and physicochemical properties of the nanocomposite membranes were enhanced, and the OH− conductivity of ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs composite membrane can reach 109.3 mS cm−1 at 80 °C, and 61.2% initial OH− conductivity was maintained in 1.0 M NaOH solution for 500 h at 60 °C. Our research proves that the nanofiller with a small size can better improve the performance of composite AEMs, and provide an efficient strategy for future research work in the design and preparation of AEMs.

A series of novel composite AEMs with enhanced conductivity and chemical stability were prepared by integrating QCDs into Im-PSU. The OH− conductivity of ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs can reach 109.3 mS cm−1 at 80 °C. Our research proves that the nanofiller with a small size can better improve the performance of composite AEMs, and provide new ideas for future research work.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Anion exchange membrane (AEM) as a vital component of alkaline anion exchange membrane fuel cell (AAEMFC) plays a critical role in blocking fuel and transporting anions in fuel cells. Therefore, the performance of AEMs directly affects the energy conversion efficiency of AAEMFCs [1–3]. Since the ionic conductivity of AEMs is directly dependent on the number of ionic conductive groups on polymer backbone, increasing the ion exchange capacity (IEC) of the AEMs is a mostly valid way to enhance the ionic conductivity in early modification strategy of AEMs. However, high IEC value will also cause excessive swelling of the polymer matrix, reducing the mechanical stability of AEMs [4–6]. In order to further improve the performance of AEMs, inorganic materials are widely used as fillers in the preparation of modified AEMs. The resulted composite AEMs not only have good mechanical properties and chemical stability, but also have improved electrochemical performance [7]. In addition, inorganic materials can also provide more modification sites. By grafting conductive groups on the surface of the inorganic materials, the ion conductivity of composite AEMs can be improved without excessive swelling [8,9]. Li et al. successfully introduced ionic liquid polymer-modified carbon nanotubes into polyphenylene ether to prepare nanohybrid AEMs [10]. It was found that the modified carbon nanotubes with a mass fraction of only 0.3% can increase the ionic conductivity and the mechanical strength of composite membrane by 95.3% and 82.9%, respectively, which proves that inorganic materials play an important role in improving the performance of AEMs. In our previous work, nanoscale quaternized mesoporous silica was introduced into polyethersulfone matrix, and it was found that the mesoporous silica material not only obviously improved the ion conductivity of the hybrid membrane in comparison with pure membrane, but also heightened the chemical and thermal stability of the resulted membranes [11]. Common inorganic materials used to modify AEMs are ZrO2 [12–14], SiO2 [15–18], TiO2 [19–21], polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane (POSS) [22,23], layered double hydroxide (LDH) [24–28], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [8,29,30], montmorillonite [31], Al2O3 [32], Fe3O4 [33] and MXenes [34]. In recent years, two-dimensional graphene and graphene oxide (GO) materials have been widely used as inorganic filler to construct organic-inorganic composite AEMs due to their special chemical properties and good stability as well as easy surface-modification [9,35–39]. Especially, GO and its modified materials can effectively improve the ionic conductivity, alcohol resistance and mechanical strength of the composite membranes, but too high doping amount easily causes agglomeration of the inorganic doped phase, thus affecting the performance of the composite membranes [37,38]. Therefore, finding a more dispersible inorganic material is of great significance for organic-inorganic hybrid AEMs.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of inorganic nanomaterials, are widely used for fluorescence detection [40–43], drug loading [44–47], photocatalysis [48,49] and other fields, due to their good optical properties, biocompatibility and small size effects. CDs have been reported to incorporate into proton exchange membranes (PEMs) to enhance the methanol resistance and proton conductivity of the nanohybrid PEMs. For example, Wu et al. successfully synthesized a multi-functional Nafion decorated carbon dots (NCDs) to modify the Nafion membrane [50]. Compared to recast Nafion, 0.5% NCDs doped Nafion hybrid PEM has a fivefold boosting in ionic conductivity and a 50% reduction in methanol permeability. Using these small nanodots instead of large-size inorganic material as a filler to modify AEMs can strengthen the dispersibility of the inorganic doping phase, thereby further improving the electrochemical properties for nanohybrid AEMs.

Herein, our aim is to use quaternized carbon dots (QCDs) as the filler to construct nanocomposite AEMs, and the influence of nanoscale filler on the performance of composite AEMs was researched in detail. The CDs with amine functional groups on the surface are first prepared from citric acid (CA) and ethylenediamine (EDA) via a hydrothermal reaction, then the surface of CDs was quaternized by the reaction of chloroglycolyltrimethylammonium chloride (GDTMAC) with amine groups of CDs. Finally, a novel type of nanocomposite AEMs were obtained by introducing different amounts of QCDs into the imidized polyethersulfone matrix (Im-PSU). It is expected that small-sized QCDs can be better dispersed into the AEMs, thereby effectively improving the performance of the composite membranes.

Polysulfone (PSU) with a weight average molecular weight of 58,000 and chloromethyl methyl ether (CMME) were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. Zinc chloride (ZnCl2), glycidyl trimethylammonium chloride (GDTMAC), 1-Methylimidazole, ethylenediamine and anhydrous citric acid were purchased from Aladdin. Others reagents were all provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent in Shanghai.

Chloromethylated polysulfone (CMPSU) was synthesized with reference to the previous method [22] as shown in Scheme 1 . The synthetic step of CMPSU was as follows: First, 5.0 g of PSU solid (drying in vacuum for 12 h before used) was dissolved in 45 mL of chloroform and stirred at 35 °C. Then, 0.85 g of ZnCl2 (drying at 100 °C in vacuum for 12 h before used) was added to the polymer solution, and after ZnCl2 was uniformly dispersed, 5.0 mL of chloromethyl ether was slowly added dropwise to the mixed solution. The reaction temperature was boosted to 45 °C, and as the stirring time was extended, the color of the reaction solution became darker and the viscosity increased. After the stirring time reached 7 h, the polymer solution was dropped into excess absolute ethanol, and the resulted precipitate was repeatedly washed with distilled water and ethanol several times. Finally, the resulting white solid (CMPSU) was obtained via vacuum drying for 12 h.

The imidized polyethersulfone (Im-PSU) was obtained by a Menshutkin reaction between the as-synthesized CMPSU and 1-methylimidazole. The specific synthesis procedure was as follows: 2.0 g of CMPSU was dissolved into 30 mL of dimethylacetamide (DMAc) to obtain a colorless clear solution before 0.75 g of 1-methylimidazole was added dropwise to the solution, followed by stirring at room temperature for 48 h. Finally, the mixed solution after the reaction was added dropwise to diethyl ether to precipitate, and the above operation was repeated three times, and dried under reduced pressure to obtain Im-PSU.

The amino groups-containing CDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal reaction according to previous literature [43]. The preparation procedure was as follows: 5.0 mmol of anhydrous citric acid solid and 5.0 mmol of ethylenediamine were added in 10 mL of distilled water, stirred uniformly, and the solution was placed in a reaction kettle, then the reaction was performed at 250 °C for 5 h. After the reaction vessel was cooled, the resulting dark red liquid was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h, followed by freeze-drying to obtain the CDs powder.

As shown in Scheme 2 , the CDs powder (0.25 g) obtained in the previous step was dispersed in 240 mL of water under ultrasound, and then 0.5 mL of GDTMAC was added before the reaction was stirred at 25 °C for 3 h. The reaction temperature was further raised to 80 °C for 6 h. Finally, the obtained reaction solution was centrifuged (9000 r min−1), and the resulting solid sample was collected and washed with ethanol, and the final QCDs were dried in vacuum at 70 °C for 12 h.

Various mass of QCDs was added in 1.5 mL DMAc and sonicated for 0.5 h to make them evenly dispersed. 0.3 g Im-PSU was dissolved into 3.2 mL DMAc at 60 °C under stirring. Subsequently, the QCDs solution was added into polymer system, and the resulting mixed solution was stirred for 10 h at 50 °C to gain a casting solution. Afterwards, the casting liquid obtained previously was degassed by a vacuum pump and poured onto a glass plate, and heated for 10 h at 50 °C. The semi-dried membranes were then transferred to a 70 °C vacuum oven for 10 h to get dry nanocomposite AEMs. The AEMs prepared above were immersed into 1.0 M KOH solution for 48 h to exchange the anions in the composite membranes with OH−, followed by washing off excess KOH with deionized water. The composite AEMs prepared in this experiment are denoted as ImPSU-x-QCDs, where x stands for the quality percentage of QCDs in the nanocomposite AEMs.

1H NMR test (600 MHz) was carried out by a Bruker Avance spectrometer in chloroform-d (CDCl3) or dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO-d6). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the samples were obtained by a Magna 560 FTIR spectrometer in a wavenumber range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were performed on a VGESCALAB 220-IXL spectrometer.

The fracture surface structure of the AEMs was acquired using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (XL-30ESEM FEG, FEI Company). Prior to observation, the nanocomposite AEMs were subjected to liquid nitrogen cooling brittle fracture to obtain a fracture surface of the composite membranes. The microstructure of the nanocomposite AEMs was investigated by a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (TEM). The membrane samples for TEM testing were dyed with PdCl4 2− ions by immersion in 2.0 × 10−2 M PdCl2 solution for 40 h, then rinsed several times with distilled water and desiccated in a vacuum oven at room temperature for 24 h. The dried AEMs were cured with epoxy resin and then cut into <100 nm thick sections with a microtome and placed on a copper mesh for observation.

The thermal stability of the AEMs was measured on a Perkin-Elmer TGA-2 thermogravimetric analyzer (heating rate: 10 °C min−1; temperature range: 30–800 °C; nitrogen atmosphere). The mechanical properties of the AEMs were first to cut into a size of 30 mm × 5 mm and measured on an Instron Model 1122 at 25 °C, 50% relative humidity (elongation rate: 5 mm min−1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion exchange capacity (IEC), water uptake (WU), swelling ratio (SR) and hydration number (λ)
ce:section.ce:para:
IEC represents the amount of a substance containing a conductive ionic group per unit mass of AEMs, and the IEC value is determined using acid-base titration method. The AEMs were first dipped into 25 mL of 0.05 M HCl solution for 48 h, respectively. Afterwards, phenolphthalein was used as an indicator, and HCl solution was titrated with 0.05 M NaOH solution, and the IEC value of the AEMs is calculated by the following formula:
IEC = (Va – Vb) × CNaOH/Wd
where Wd (g) represents the weight of the dry membrane, Va (mL) represents the volume of the blank sample, and Vb (mL) refers to the volume of the alkaline solution consumed for testing, and CNaOH (mol L−1) is the concentration of the alkaline solution.

The membrane samples were immerged in distilled water for 48 h at room temperature, and the water molecules adsorbed on the membrane surface were quickly wiped off after the membranes were taken out and the weight was weighed. The membranes were then placed under vacuum for 12 h at 80 °C and their weight was weighed. The WU of the AEMs is determined according to the following formula:
WU (%) = [(Ww − Wd)/Wd] × 100%
where Ww (g) and Wd (g) are the weight of the wet and dry AEMs.

SR of AEMs can be obtained according to the following equation:
SR (L%) = [(Lw − Ld)/Ld] × 100%
where Lw (cm) and Ld (cm) are the length of the wet and dry AEMs.

Using the WU and IEC values of the membranes obtained above, the hydration number (λ) of the AEMs can be calculated by the following formula:
λ = WU (%) × 10/(IEC × 18)

The ionic conductivity of the AEMs is mainly determined by the four-electrode AC impedance method. First, the electrochemical impedance of the AEMs (4 cm × 1 cm) was tested on an electrochemical workstation. The frequency range was set to 1 Hz–100 Hz, and the test temperature was selected to be 25 °C, 40 °C, 60 °C and 80 °C. The anion conductivity of the AEMs is calculated from the following formula:
σ = L/RA
where L (cm) represents the distance between the two electrodes, R (Ω) is the AC impedance value of the membranes, and A (cm2) represents the cross-sectional area of the membranes.

The apparent activation energy (Ea) of ionic conductivity can be determined according to the following formula:
Ea = −b × R
where b is the slope of ln σ∼1000/T curve and R is the gas constant (8.314 kJ mol−1).

Alkaline stability of the AEMs was investigated through immersing them into a 1.0 M NaOH solution at 60 °C. And the ionic conductivity at 80 °C was measured for the AEMs obtained after different alkaline treatment times.

Oxidative stability test was performed by immerging the AEMs (1 cm × 3 cm) into Fenton's reagent (4 ppm FeCl2 dissolved in 3% H2O2) at 80 °C, and the stability was characterized by recording the time when AEMs began to break (τ1) and broke into pieces (τ2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: 1H NMR spectra of CMPSU and Im-PSU
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 a shows the 1H NMR spectra of the PSU and CMPSU polymers. The number of chloromethyl groups on CMPSU chains was determined to be 1.25 based on previous work [22]. Fig. 1b is the 1H NMR spectrum of Im-PSU. It can be seen that the characteristic peak of chloromethyl hydrogen at 4.56 ppm disappeared, and a new characteristic peak appeared at 5.37 ppm, which is attributed to the typical peak of N–CH2–Ar [51]. At the same time, characteristic peaks of 1-methylimidazole groups appeared at 3.67 ppm, 7.9–8.0 ppm, and 9.12 ppm, respectively, indicating that the imidazole groups have been successfully functionalized onto the polymer side chains [52]. Based on the integrated area at 4.56 and 5.37 ppm, the degree of imidization of the Im-PSU can be calculated to be about 93.7%.

A comparison of FTIR spectra for the carbon dots before and after the quaternization reaction is given in Fig. 2 a. The stretching vibration peaks of hydroxyl groups and amine groups can be observed at 3400 cm−1. The characteristic peak at 2930 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of –CH2–, while the N–H bending vibration and C–NH–C stretching vibration appeared at around 1570 and 1126 cm−1, respectively [43]. A new signal peak ascribed to quaternary ammonium groups can be observed at 1650 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of QCDs, indicating that the quaternization reaction has been successfully carried out for the carbon dots [38]. The 1H NMR spectra of CDs and QCDs are shown in Fig. 2c. The basic structure of CDs can be determined according to literature report [43]. As for QCDs, the characteristic methyl peak (a) derived from GDTMAC part appeared at 3.15 ppm, and a new characteristic peak b appeared at 4.0 ppm, indicating that QCDs have been synthesized successfully.

Fig. 2d shows the TGA curves for CDs and QCDs. A weight loss below 200 °C is attributed to the evaporation of absorbed water in the samples. Both CDs and QCDs exhibited significant weight loss between 250 and 600 °C due to thermal decomposition of surface organic functional groups and further carbonization of carbon dots [53]. However, QCDs has a faster weight loss process than that of CDs between 300 and 500 °C with lower residues, which is mainly attributed to introduction of thermally unstable GDTMAC part containing quaternary ammonium groups on the surface of QCDs [39]. Combined with TGA analysis, we can calculate the amount of GDTMAC moiety grafted onto the surface of QCDs to be 9.64 wt%.

Fig. 3 a,b provide the TEM and HRTEM images of QCDs. Before the TEM test, we first dispersed the QCDs in deionized water and then dropped the dispersion onto a carbon support membrane for testing. It can be clearly seen that the average diameter of QCDs prepared by us is only 3–4 nm (Fig. 3c), and all QCDs are distributed very uniformly without obvious aggregation.

The chemical composition of QCDs was further analyzed by XPS. As shown in the full range XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d), there are three typical peaks corresponding to C (285.3 eV), N (399.2 eV) and O (531.8 eV). In addition, the nitrogen content in the QCDs (6.48%) was higher than that in the CDs (3.99%) due to the surface-quaternized modification of CDs. The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 4 a and d) of C 1s for CDs and QCDs display four peaks at 284.6, 285.9, 287.2 and 288.4 eV, which are corresponding to C=C/C–C, C–N, C–O and C=N/C=O groups [54]. As deposited in Fig. 4c and f, the O 1s spectra of CDs and QCDs show two peaks at 531.5 and 532.9 eV designated as C=O and C–O–C/C–OH bonds, respectively [55]. For the high-resolution spectra of N 1s of CDs (Fig. 4b), the peaks of 399.8 and 401.0 eV are designated to the signals of the C–N–C and N–H groups [43], respectively. Furthermore, we found that the typical peak (401.0 eV) belonging to the N–H groups disappeared in the high-resolution spectrum of N 1s of QCDs (Fig. 4e). This result also demonstrated that the amino groups on the surface of the CDs have reacted with the epoxy groups of GDTMAC to generate QCDs.

The FTIR spectra of the AEMs are illustrated in Fig. 2b. The peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to the imidazole groups of Im-PSU matrix, which is consistent with previous report [51]. By comparing the IR spectra of different nanocomposite AEMs, no new characteristic peaks of QCDs appeared in AEMs because the content of QCDs in the membranes is very low. So it is difficult to observe the information of the doping phase, and there is no chemical bond between QCDs and polymer matrix, and with increasing content of QCDs, the characteristic peak intensity of the nanocomposite AEMs is slightly weakened, indicating that the addition of carbon dots could weaken the infrared characteristic peak signal intensity of the composite membranes [56].

Fig. 6 is the cross-sectional morphology of various AEMs. The cross section for the original Im-PSU membrane exhibited a smooth surface, and as the QCDs content increased, the roughness of the composite membranes increased significantly. It is worth noting that all nanocomposite AEMs did not undergo significant phase separation at a submicroscopic level when introducing the small-sized QCDs, as both Im-PSU and QCDs have similar conductive ionic groups and strong interaction, providing higher compatibility between filler and polymer matrix [9,36].

All the membranes display the decomposition in three main stages as illustrated in Fig. 7 a. The first stage appeared between 50 and 220 °C, where the weight loss was mainly caused by the volatilization of water and solvent adsorbed on the surface of the membrane materials. The second stage of thermal weight loss mainly occurred between 220 and 380 °C on account of the thermolysis of the imidazolium groups on the polymer chains. The third stage of thermal weight loss appeared above 380 °C and was caused by thermolysis of the polymer skeleton. The TGA results showed that all composite membranes had good thermal stability, and the introduction of QCDs had no significant effect on the thermal properties of polymer matrix [57].

Table 1 lists the mechanical properties of pure Im-PSU and nanocomposite AEMs. All samples were measured at 25 °C in a dry environment. As displayed in Fig. 7b, with the increasing content of QCDs, on the whole, Young's modulus of the nanocomposite AEMs increased from 970 to 1600 MPa, the tensile strength raised to 70.1 from 50 MPa, at the same time, the elongation at break raised to 15.5 from 7.3%. Above results demonstrated that QCDs could enhance the mechanical properties of the nanocomposite AEMs to some extent [11].

ce:section.ce:section-title: IEC, WU, SR and hydration number(λ)
ce:section.ce:para: As illustrated in Table 2 , the IEC value of the nanocomposite AEMs increased from 2.13 to 2.31 mmol g−1 with the increasing content of QCDs, indicating that the as-prepared QCDs can effectively increase the number of ionic conductive groups in the nanocomposite AEMs [58]. The increase in λ is caused by an increase in the IEC value of the membranes. It is clear from Table 2 that as the QCDs content increased, the λ value increased from 10.4 to 13.4. Since the imidazole groups are selected as the ion conductive groups of the polymer matrix, the WU of the nanocomposite AEMs is generally high, which ensures that the AEMs have good ion conductivity [26,36]. It also was found from Table 2 that as increasing the content of QCDs, the WU and SR of the nanocomposite AEMs further increased, which is largely dependent on the quaternary ammonium structure on the surface of QCDs. When the doping amount of QCDs is only 0.5 wt%, the WU of the nanocomposite AEMs at 80 °C is nearly 10% higher than that of the Im-PSU membrane. This illustrates that the QCDs prepared by us can produce a significant effect on the water uptake of the AEMs with only a small doping amount, which may help to further improve the ion conductivity of AEMs.

As the most important parameter of AEMs, the level of ionic conductivity directly affects the performance of AEMs in fuel cells. Herein, the ionic conductivity of the nanocomposite AEMs was improved by introducing QCDs into the imidized polyethersulfone matrix. It can be seen from Fig. 8 a that as the content of QCDs increased, the OH− conductivity of the nanocomposite AEMs at 25 °C was higher than that of the pure Im-PSU membrane. For example, the OH− conductivity (31.9 mS cm−1) of the ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs was nearly twofold higher than that (16.4 mS cm−1) of Im-PSU membrane, and this value at 80 °C increased from 86.9 mS cm−1 for Im-PSU membrane to 109.3 mS cm−1 for ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs. This is mainly caused by two reasons: first, the surface of the QCDs contains a large number of quaternary ammonium groups which increased the number of conductive ionic groups of the nanocomposite AEMs, and further enhanced the ionic conductivity [29]. Second, since the IEC and WU of the nanocomposite AEMs were enhanced with the addition of QCDs (Fig. 8b), the ion transport performance of the nanocomposite AEMs was improved as illustrated in Scheme 3 [59]. Moreover, Fig. 5a–c also demonstrates that the addition of QCDs can modulate the ion transport channel. This is because that the electrostatic interaction between the quaternary ammonium groups on QCDs surface and the imidazolium groups on the polymer backbone facilitates the formation of effective ion transport channels [22,38]. As the QCDs content reached 2.5%, the WU of the nanocomposite membrane increased, but the OH− conductivity decreased slightly (see Fig. 8c), indicating that the aggregation of QCDs may begin to occur in the nanocomposite membrane at this level. As discussed above, when the QCDs content reached 5.0%, the hydrophilic phase region of the composite membrane becomes larger (see Fig. 5d), but the OH− conductivity of the composite membrane is seriously degraded (see Fig. 8c), which proves that aggregation of QCDs occurs and this aggregation phenomenon hinders the transmission of OH− [60]. Compared with the previous work [59], a small amount of QCDs doping can significantly regulate the ion transport capacity of the composite membranes.

As shown in Fig. 8d, as the content of QCDs increases, the dependence of the OH− conductivity of the nanocomposite AEMs on temperature follows typical Arrhenius behavior. In this work, the activation energy of the OH− conduction of the membrane is between 15.3 and 27.1 kJ mol−1, which is in the range reported in the prior literature with similar study [61]. We also compared the hydroxide conductivity of the ImPSU-x-QCDs membranes at 80 °C as a function of the IEC value with some previously reported AEMs (eg ImPPO/IL-GO-0.5% [36], QAPPO-8%-IL1-SiO2 [18], QPPO/QCNC (2%) [8], QPPO-Im-3%LDH-5000 V cm−1 [26], membrane C [17], QPAES/10% nano-ZrO2 [13], QSIBS/GOA-0.50 wt% [35]). As shown in Fig. 9 , it was found that ImPSU-0.5%-QCDs, ImPSU-1.0%-QCDs and ImPSU-2.5%-QCDs membranes showed higher OH− conductivity than other membranes at similar IEC levels. Therefore, QCDs modified composite membranes are worth considering to enhance the ionic conductivity of AEMs.

Oxidative stability represents the oxidation resistance of AEMs under actual battery operating conditions [62]. As shown in Table 2, as the content of QCDs increased, the oxidative stability of the nanocomposite AEMs was improved as compared with the pure Im-PSU membrane, and the nanocomposite AEMs were finally able to remain in the Fenton reagent for 47 h (τ2). It is shown that the QCDs as filler not only can improve the electrochemical performance of the nanocomposite AEMs, but also enhance the oxidation resistance of the polymer matrix. In addition, the nanocomposite AEMs we prepared have good oxidative stability as compared to previous reports [63,64].

The alkaline stability of the ImPSU-x-QCDs membrane was estimated by studying the change in ionic conductivity after soaking in a 1.0 M NaOH solution at 60 °C. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the initial sharp drop in ionic conductivity was within the first 200 h, and then the ionic conductivity tended to level off. Interestingly, the ImPSU-x-QCDs membranes maintained a relatively higher ionic conductivity compared to the pure membrane after 500 h of testing (see Fig. 10b). A reasonable explanation is that QCDs act as fillers to hinder the movement of the polymer segments, inhibiting the attack of oxygen radicals on the polymer backbone and the introduction of QCDs can reduce the SN2 nucleophilic substitution of the OH− on the imidazolium groups at the C2 position [36,65]. In addition, the carboxyl groups on the surface of QCDs can interact with the imidazolium cation, which causes QCDs to surround the imidazolium groups, thereby hindering the attack of OH− on the imidazolium groups.

This paper is devoted to the synthesis of small-sized nano-carbon dot materials functionalized by surface quaternary ammonium groups, and the as-prepared QCDs were directly blended with imidized polysulfone matrix to fabricate novel composite AEMs. The preliminary results showed that the as-prepared QCDs with small size (3–4 nm) could effectively improve the OH− conductivity and physicochemical properties of composite AEMs. It was also confirmed by TEM that a small amount of QCDs doping can significantly control the ion transport capacity of composite AEMs. In future work, we will continue to optimize the surface functional structure of carbon nanodots and the structure of anionic polymer electrolytes. It is desirable to further increase the doping content and obtain a composite membrane with a crosslinked structure while maintaining good compatibility between the two components for heightening the overall performance of the AEMs. Our work also proves that the small sized nanofiller can better improve the performance of composite AEMs, and provide new ideas for future research.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21574017).
